sourceQuestion:
In the worst case, the number of comparisons needed to search a single linked list of length n for a given element is

A. log_2(n)
B. n/2
C. log_2(n) - 1
D. n

parsed.stem: In the worst case, the number of comparisons needed to search a single linked list of length n for a given element is

sourceAnswer: B. the elements in the list are sorted in non-decreasing order of data value

options.text: the list is empty or has exactly one element
the elements in the list are sorted in non-decreasing order of data value
the elements in the list are sorted in non-increasing order of data value
not all elements in the list have the same data value

sourceQuestion:
A circularly linked list is used to represent a Queue. A single variable p is used to access the Queue. To which node should p point such that both the operations enQueue and deQueue can be performed in constant time?

A. rear node
B. front node
C. not possible with a single pointer
D. node next to front

parsed.stem: A circularly linked list is used to represent a Queue. A single variable p is used to access the Queue. To which node should p point such that both the operations enQueue and deQueue can be performed in constant time?

options.text: not possible with a single pointer

sourceQuestion:
Suppose each set is represented as a linked list with elements in arbitrary order. Which of the operations among union, intersection, membership, cardinality will be the slowest?

A. union only
B. intersection, membership
C. membership, cardinality
D. union, intersection

parsed.stem: Suppose each set is represented as a linked list with elements in arbitrary order. Which of the operations among union, intersection, membership, cardinality will be the slowest?

sourceAnswer: A. q->next = NULL; p->next = head; head = p;

options.text: q->next = NULL; p->next = head; head = p;
q->next = NULL; head = p; p->next = head;
head = p; p->next = q; q->next = NULL;
q->next = NULL; head = p; p->next = head;

sourceQuestion:
N items are stored in a sorted doubly linked list. For a delete operation, a pointer is provided to the record to be deleted. For a decrease-key operation, a pointer is provided to the record on which the operation is to be performed.

An algorithm performs the following operations on the list in this order: Theta(N) delete, O(log N) insert, O(log N) find, and Theta(N) decrease-key. What is the time complexity of all these operations put together?

A. O(log^2 N)
B. O(N)
C. O(N^2)
D. Theta(N^2 log N)

parsed.stem:
N items are stored in a sorted doubly linked list. For a delete operation, a pointer is provided to the record to be deleted. For a decrease-key operation, a pointer is provided to the record on which the operation is to be performed.

An algorithm performs the following operations on the list in this order: Theta(N) delete, O(log N) insert, O(log N) find, and Theta(N) decrease-key. What is the time complexity of all these operations put together?

sourceAnswer: B. either cause a null pointer dereference or append list m to the end of list n.

options.text: append list m to the end of list n for all inputs.
either cause a null pointer dereference or append list m to the end of list n.
cause a null pointer dereference for all inputs.
append list n to the end of list m for all inputs.

sourceQuestion:
What is the worst case time complexity of inserting n elements into an empty linked list, if the linked list needs to be maintained in sorted order?

A. Theta(n)
B. Theta(n log n)
C. Theta(n^2)
D. Theta(1)

parsed.stem: What is the worst case time complexity of inserting n elements into an empty linked list, if the linked list needs to be maintained in sorted order?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the problem of reversing a singly linked list. To take an example, given the linked list below,

1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 -> NULL

the reversed linked list should look like:

5 -> 4 -> 3 -> 2 -> 1 -> NULL

Which one of the following statements is TRUE about the time complexity of algorithms that solve the above problem in O(1) space?

A. The best algorithm for the problem takes Theta(n) time in the worst case.
B. The best algorithm for the problem takes Theta(n log n) time in the worst case.
C. The best algorithm for the problem takes Theta(n^2) time in the worst case.
D. It is not possible to reverse a singly linked list in O(1) space.
sourceAnswer: A. The best algorithm for the problem takes Theta(n) time in the worst case.

parsed.stem:
Consider the problem of reversing a singly linked list. To take an example, given the linked list below,

1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 -> NULL

the reversed linked list should look like:

5 -> 4 -> 3 -> 2 -> 1 -> NULL

Which one of the following statements is TRUE about the time complexity of algorithms that solve the above problem in O(1) space?

options.text: The best algorithm for the problem takes Theta(n) time in the worst case.
The best algorithm for the problem takes Theta(n log n) time in the worst case.
The best algorithm for the problem takes Theta(n^2) time in the worst case.
It is not possible to reverse a singly linked list in O(1) space.

sourceQuestion:
Let SLLdel be a function that deletes a node in a singly-linked list given a pointer to the node and a pointer to the head of the list. Similarly, let DLLdel be another function that deletes a node in a doubly-linked list given a pointer to the node and a pointer to the head of the list. Let n denote the number of nodes in each list. Which one of the following choices is TRUE about the worst-case time complexity of SLLdel and DLLdel?

A. SLLdel is O(1) and DLLdel is O(log n)
B. SLLdel is O(n) and DLLdel is O(log n)
C. SLLdel is O(n) and DLLdel is O(1)
D. SLLdel is O(1) and DLLdel is O(1)
sourceAnswer: C. SLLdel is O(n) and DLLdel is O(1)

parsed.stem: Let SLLdel be a function that deletes a node in a singly-linked list given a pointer to the node and a pointer to the head of the list. Similarly, let DLLdel be another function that deletes a node in a doubly-linked list given a pointer to the node and a pointer to the head of the list. Let n denote the number of nodes in each list. Which one of the following choices is TRUE about the worst-case time complexity of SLLdel and DLLdel?

options.text: SLLdel is O(1) and DLLdel is O(log n)
SLLdel is O(n) and DLLdel is O(log n)
SLLdel is O(n) and DLLdel is O(1)
SLLdel is O(1) and DLLdel is O(1)

sourceQuestion:
Let P be a singly linked list. Let Q be the pointer to an intermediate node x in the list. What is the worst-case time complexity of the best-known algorithm to delete the node x from the list?

A. O(n)
B. O(log^2 n)
C. O(log n)
D. O(1)

parsed.stem: Let P be a singly linked list. Let Q be the pointer to an intermediate node x in the list. What is the worst-case time complexity of the best-known algorithm to delete the node x from the list?

sourceQuestion:
A priority queue Q is used to implement a stack that stores characters. PUSH(C) is implemented as INSERT(Q, C, K) where K is an appropriate integer key chosen by the implementation. POP is implemented as DELETEMIN. For a sequence of operations, the keys chosen are in

A. non-increasing order
B. non-decreasing order
C. strictly increasing order
D. strictly decreasing order

parsed.stem: A priority queue Q is used to implement a stack that stores characters. PUSH(C) is implemented as INSERT(Q, C, K) where K is an appropriate integer key chosen by the implementation. POP is implemented as DELETEMIN. For a sequence of operations, the keys chosen are in

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following statements:
i. First-in-first out types of computations are efficiently supported by STACKS.
ii. Implementing LISTS on linked lists is more efficient than implementing LISTS on an array for almost all the basic LIST operations.
iii. Implementing QUEUES on a circular array is more efficient than implementing QUEUES on a linear array with two indices.
iv. Last-in-first out types of computations are efficiently supported by QUEUES.

Which of the following is correct?
A. (i) and (ii) are true
B. (ii) and (iii) are true
C. (iii) and (iv) are true
D. (ii) and (iv) are true

parsed.stem:
Consider the following statements:
i. First-in-first out types of computations are efficiently supported by STACKS.
ii. Implementing LISTS on linked lists is more efficient than implementing LISTS on an array for almost all the basic LIST operations.
iii. Implementing QUEUES on a circular array is more efficient than implementing QUEUES on a linear array with two indices.
iv. Last-in-first out types of computations are efficiently supported by QUEUES.

Which of the following is correct?

sourceQuestion:
What is the minimum number of stacks of size n required to implement a queue of size n?
A. One
B. Two
C. Three
D. Four

parsed.stem: What is the minimum number of stacks of size n required to implement a queue of size n?

sourceAnswer: B. n + m <= x < 2n and 2m <= y <= 2n

options.text: n + m <= x < 2n and 2m <= y <= n + m

sourceQuestion:
Suppose a circular queue of capacity (n-1) elements is implemented with an array of n elements. Assume that the insertion and deletion operations are carried out using REAR and FRONT as array index variables, respectively. Initially, REAR = FRONT = 0. The conditions to detect queue full and queue empty are:

A. full: (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT
   empty: REAR == FRONT
B. full: (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT
   empty: (FRONT+1) mod n == REAR
C. full: REAR == FRONT
   empty: (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT
D. full: (FRONT+1) mod n == REAR
   empty: REAR == FRONT
sourceAnswer:
A. full: (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT
   empty: REAR == FRONT

parsed.stem: Suppose a circular queue of capacity (n-1) elements is implemented with an array of n elements. Assume that the insertion and deletion operations are carried out using REAR and FRONT as array index variables, respectively. Initially, REAR = FRONT = 0. The conditions to detect queue full and queue empty are:

options.text: full: (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT empty: REAR == FRONT
full: (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT empty: (FRONT+1) mod n == REAR
full: REAR == FRONT empty: (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT
full: (FRONT+1) mod n == REAR empty: REAR == FRONT

sourceQuestion:
A queue is implemented using an array such that ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE operations are performed efficiently. Which one of the following statements is CORRECT (n refers to the number of items in the queue)?

A. Both operations can be performed in O(1) time.
B. At most one operation can be performed in O(1) time but the worst case time for the other operation will be Omega(n).
C. The worst case time complexity for both operations will be Omega(n).
D. Worst case time for at least one of the operations will be Omega(log n).
sourceAnswer: A. Both operations can be performed in O(1) time.

parsed.stem: A queue is implemented using an array such that ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE operations are performed efficiently. Which one of the following statements is CORRECT (n refers to the number of items in the queue)?

options.text: Both operations can be performed in O(1) time.
At most one operation can be performed in O(1) time but the worst case time for the other operation will be Omega(n).
The worst case time complexity for both operations will be Omega(n).
Worst case time for at least one of the operations will be Omega(log n).

sourceQuestion:
A circular queue has been implemented using a singly linked list where each node consists of a value and a single pointer pointing to the next node. We maintain exactly two external pointers FRONT and REAR pointing to the front node and the rear node of the queue, respectively. Which of the following statements is/are CORRECT for such a circular queue, so that insertion and deletion operations can be performed in O(1) time?

I. Next pointer of front node points to the rear node.
II. Next pointer of rear node points to the front node.

A. I only
B. II only
C. Both (I) and (II)
D. Neither (I) nor (II)

parsed.stem:
A circular queue has been implemented using a singly linked list where each node consists of a value and a single pointer pointing to the next node. We maintain exactly two external pointers FRONT and REAR pointing to the front node and the rear node of the queue, respectively. Which of the following statements is/are CORRECT for such a circular queue, so that insertion and deletion operations can be performed in O(1) time?

I. Next pointer of front node points to the rear node.
II. Next pointer of rear node points to the front node.

sourceQuestion:
A queue is implemented using a non-circular singly linked list. The queue has a head pointer and a tail pointer, as shown in the figure. Let n denote the number of nodes in the queue. Let 'enqueue' be implemented by inserting a new node at the head, and 'dequeue' be implemented by deletion of a node from the tail.

Head -> [node] -> [node] -> ... -> [node] -> NULL
                                    (tail)

Which one of the following is the time complexity of the most time-efficient implementation of 'enqueue' and 'dequeue', respectively, for this data structure?

A. Theta(1), Theta(1)
B. Theta(1), Theta(n)
C. Theta(n), Theta(1)
D. Theta(n), Theta(n)

parsed.stem:
A queue is implemented using a non-circular singly linked list. The queue has a head pointer and a tail pointer, as shown in the figure. Let n denote the number of nodes in the queue. Let 'enqueue' be implemented by inserting a new node at the head, and 'dequeue' be implemented by deletion of a node from the tail.

Head -> [node] -> [node] -> ... -> [node] -> NULL
                                    (tail)

Which one of the following is the time complexity of the most time-efficient implementation of 'enqueue' and 'dequeue', respectively, for this data structure?

sourceAnswer: B. Reverses the order of the elements in the queue Q

options.text: Reverses the order of the elements in the queue Q
Deletes the element at the front of the queue Q and inserts it at the rear keeping the other elements in the same order

sourceQuestion:
The following sequence of operations is performed on a stack:
PUSH(10), PUSH(20), POP, PUSH(10), PUSH(30), POP, POP, POP, PUSH(20), POP

The sequence of values popped out is:
A. 20, 10, 20, 10, 30
B. 20, 30, 10, 10, 20
C. 10, 20, 10, 20, 30
D. 20, 30, 10, 20, 10

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following permutations can be obtained in the output (in the same order) using a stack, assuming that the input is the sequence 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 in that order?
A. 3, 4, 5, 1, 2
B. 3, 4, 5, 2, 1
C. 1, 2, 5, 3, 4
D. 5, 4, 3, 2, 1

parsed.stem: Which of the following permutations can be obtained in the output (in the same order) using a stack, assuming that the input is the sequence 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 in that order?

sourceQuestion:
The postfix expression for the infix expression A + B * (C + D) / F + D * E is:
A. AB CD + * F / DE * + +
B. ABCD + * F / DE * + +
C. A + BCD + * F / DE * +
D. A + BCD + * F / DE * + +

parsed.stem: The postfix expression for the infix expression A + B * (C + D) / F + D * E is:

sourceQuestion:
Let S be a stack of size n >= 1. Starting with the empty stack, suppose we push the first n natural numbers in sequence, and then perform n pop operations. Assume that Push and Pop operations take X seconds each, and Y seconds elapse between the end of one such stack operation and the start of the next operation. The average stack-life of an element (the time elapsed from when an element is pushed until it is popped) is:
A. n(X + Y)
B. 3Y + 2X
C. n(X + Y) - X
D. Y + 2X

parsed.stem: Let S be a stack of size n >= 1. Starting with the empty stack, suppose we push the first n natural numbers in sequence, and then perform n pop operations. Assume that Push and Pop operations take X seconds each, and Y seconds elapse between the end of one such stack operation and the start of the next operation. The average stack-life of an element (the time elapsed from when an element is pushed until it is popped) is:

sourceQuestion:
A single array A[1..MAXSIZE] is used to implement two stacks. The two stacks grow from opposite ends of the array. Variables top1 and top2 (top1 < top2) point to the location of the topmost element in each of the stacks. If the space is to be used efficiently, the condition for "stack full" is:
A. (top1 = MAXSIZE/2) and (top2 = MAXSIZE/2 + 1)
B. top1 + top2 = MAXSIZE
C. (top1 = MAXSIZE/2) or (top2 = MAXSIZE/2)
D. top1 + 1 = top2

Variables top1 and top2 (top1 < top2) point to the location of the topmost element in each of the stacks. If the space is to be used efficiently, the condition for "stack full" is:

sourceQuestion:
The best data structure to check whether an arithmetic expression has balanced parentheses is a:
A. queue
B. stack
C. tree
D. list

parsed.stem: The best data structure to check whether an arithmetic expression has balanced parentheses is a:

sourceQuestion:
Suppose a stack implementation supports an instruction REVERSE, which reverses the order of elements on the stack, in addition to the PUSH and POP instructions. Which one of the following statements is TRUE (with respect to this modified stack)?
A. A queue cannot be implemented using this stack.
B. A queue can be implemented where ENQUEUE takes a single instruction and DEQUEUE takes a sequence of instructions.
C. A queue can be implemented where ENQUEUE takes a sequence of instructions and DEQUEUE takes a single instruction.
D. A queue can be implemented where both ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE take a single instruction each.
sourceAnswer: B. A queue can be implemented where ENQUEUE takes a single instruction and DEQUEUE takes a sequence of instructions.

parsed.stem: Suppose a stack implementation supports an instruction REVERSE, which reverses the order of elements on the stack, in addition to the PUSH and POP instructions. Which one of the following statements is TRUE (with respect to this modified stack)?

options.text: A queue cannot be implemented using this stack.
A queue can be implemented where ENQUEUE takes a single instruction and DEQUEUE takes a sequence of instructions.
A queue can be implemented where ENQUEUE takes a sequence of instructions and DEQUEUE takes a single instruction.
A queue can be implemented where both ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE take a single instruction each.
